title: Plan-and-Execute vs ReAct
concept: Contrasting macro upfront planning with micro step-by-step reaction; matching strategy to task length.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: what does an agent do with 'dynamic plan revision' when a worker fails mid-task?

options.text: It quits the whole task immediately
It updates the remaining steps to react to the surprise
It ignores the failure and keeps going

exercises.explain: Dynamic plan revision means the agent rewrites its leftover steps when something unexpected happens, instead of blindly following the original plan or giving up.

cards.title: Two Ways to Get Things Done
cards.body: Some tasks you plan out fully before starting. Others you figure out one step at a time as you go. Agents work the same way!

cards.title: Plan-and-Execute vs ReAct
cards.body: Plan-and-Execute makes the whole plan upfront, then does each step. ReAct thinks and acts one small step at a time, deciding the next move after seeing each result.

cards.title: Road Trip vs City Walk
cards.body: A long road trip needs a map planned in advance (Plan-and-Execute). Wandering a new city, you just glance around and pick your next turn (ReAct).

cards.title: Booking a Trip
cards.body: Planning a 7-step vacation booking? Plan-and-Execute keeps you organized. Answering one quick 'what's the weather?' question? ReAct is faster and simpler.

cards.title: Not Always Better
cards.body: Myth: Plan-and-Execute is always smarter. Truth: for short, simple tasks it's overkill. ReAct's step-by-step style wins when the path is unclear or short.

cards.title: Macro vs Micro Thinking
cards.body: Plan-and-Execute thinks big upfront. ReAct thinks small and often.

left.text: Map the whole plan, then execute each step

right.text: Think, act, look, repeat one step at a time

exercises.prompt: Which task is a better fit for Plan-and-Execute?

options.text: A long 10-step research report with clear stages

exercises.explain: Long, multi-step tasks with clear stages benefit from planning ahead. Short or simple tasks don't need a big upfront plan.

exercises.prompt: True or False: ReAct decides its next action after seeing the result of the last one.
exercises.explain: That's exactly how ReAct works: it reacts step by step, choosing each next move based on the latest result instead of planning everything upfront.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about matching strategy to task length.
exercises.sentence: For short, unpredictable tasks, the ReAct step-by-step style is often the better choice.
exercises.explain: ReAct works in a step-by-step way, reacting to each result, which suits short or unpredictable tasks.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of choosing a strategy was written by an AI?

options.text: Match the strategy to the task: long, structured tasks favor upfront planning, while short or uncertain tasks favor reacting step by step.
just use planing lol its always beter trust me
idk pick whatever feels right, doesnt matter really

exercises.explain: The AI answer is clear, balanced, and correctly matches strategy to task length, unlike the sloppy or careless human-style notes.

exercises.prompt: You build an agent for a long 12-step data pipeline with clear stages, plus a second agent for quick, one-off user questions. How should you match strategies?

options.text: Plan-and-Execute for the pipeline, ReAct for quick questions
ReAct for the pipeline, Plan-and-Execute for quick questions

exercises.explain: Match strategy to task length and structure. Long, structured tasks like a 12-step pipeline benefit from macro upfront planning (Plan-and-Execute), while short, unpredictable questions are handled faster with micro step-by-step reaction (ReAct). Neither is universally 'best'—the right pick depends on the job.
